public int[] flip() {
    int x = random.nextInt(total);
    // find actual val
    int val = x;
    if (map.containsKey(x)) {
        val = map.get(x);
    }
    // now, take the last element (total - 1)
    int last = total - 1;
    // put the last element into map for x
    map.put(x, last);
    // decrease total
    total--;
    // convert val to 2D index
    int[] res = {val / n, val % n};
    return res;
}

In Java:

Implement the variables m, n, total, map, and a Random instance.

class Solution {
    private int m;
    private int n;
    private int total;
    private Map<Integer, Integer> map;
    private Random rand;

    public Solution(int m, int n) {
        this.m = m;
        this.n = n;
        this.total = m * n;
        this.map = new HashMap<>();
        this.rand = new Random();
    }
    
    public int[] flip() {
        int x = rand.nextInt(total);
        int val;
        if (map.containsKey(x)) {
            val = map.get(x);
        } else {
            val = x;
        }
        int last = total - 1;
        map.put(x, last);
        total--;
        int[] res = {val / n, val % n};
        return res;
    }
    
    public void reset() {
        map.clear();
        total = m * n;
    }
}

And then, the last value will be processed in the next steps.

This approach ensures that all positions are considered exactly once, and each flip is O(1) time.

solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    private int m;
    private int n;
    private int total;
    private Map<Integer, Integer> map;
    private Random rand;

    public Solution(int m, int n) {
        this.m = m;
        this.n = n;
        this.total = m * n;
        this.map = new HashMap<>();
        this.rand = new Random();
    }
    
    public int[] flip() {
        int x = rand.nextInt(total);
        int val = map.getOrDefault(x, x);
        int last = total - 1;
        map.put(x, last);
        total--;
        return new int[]{val / n, val % n};
    }
    
    public void reset() {
        map.clear();
        total = m * n;
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(1)
space complexity: O(k) where k is the number of flip() calls
energy implications: LOW
explanation: This solution uses a hash map to track flipped positions and maps random indices to available cells efficiently. It ensures each flip is O(1) time with minimal random function calls. The key idea is to simulate removing an element from an array by swapping with the last element and tracking swaps in a hash map. Reset is O(1) as it only clears the map. Space is proportional to the number of flips, which is acceptable given constraints.
